programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Colored stone inlay art: Transformation matters
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: The Story of Flying Trident
programme.desc: Feicha, or a flying trident, is a type of performance where you balance a revolving trident on your body. In Chinese history, the trident was one of the 18 main weapons of war, but now it has become more of an acrobatic act. For thousands of years, people in China have practiced it as a performance and also as a way to keep fit.\nIt seems like the trident is stuck to the person’s body like a magnet, but that effect is achieved by tensing and relaxing muscles at the right time so that the movement of the trident is channeled in the right direction—be it around the hands, neck, or back. Otherwise, its energy will turn against the performer.

programme.title: Awakening Lion
programme.desc: Xingshi, or literally “awakened lion,” is a name for the southern style of the lion dance. Historically, it evolved from a court dance in the Tang Dynasty almost 1,200 years ago. With the southward migration of the population from the central plains, lion dance culture was introduced to the south. In the Ming Dynasty around 400 years ago, xingshi appeared in Guangdong and was then widely spread throughout southern China and even overseas. Lion dance has become a very important part of Chinese culture.

programme.title: Foodwise: Beijing
programme.desc: From the local delicacies of Beijing's Ox Street to latest hangouts and contemporary cuisine of the Chinese capital, hosts Tianran He and Eva May will give you a true taste of Beijing, and the fascinating stories and people behind them.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of Tibetan antelopes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise: Beijing's Hutongs
programme.desc: The Hutongs in Beijing are considered the city's blood vessels, as these traditional alleyways make up the DNA of the capital for its rich history and culture.\nIn this episode, Tianran He and Eva May zigzag their way through the Hutongs and the Forbidden City for a mouth-watering experience full of ancient and modern delicacies. Starring German designer Katrin, Russian artist Liuba and photographer Feng Yu from Beijing, our hosts are taking you along a journey that features fried noodles, dim sum, pancakes and co.

programme.title: Foodwise: Changsha
programme.desc: In this episode, our hosts Tianran He and Eva May embark on a journey to Changsha in Hunan Province.?They taste their way through the local cuisine, discover the city's latest art and fashion trends, and find out why this place is one of China's \"influencer hotspots.

programme.title: Foodwise：Qingdao
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May venture to Qingdao, China's beer mecca by the sea. With red tiles and green trees that make up the city's stunning scenery, our hosts embark on another foodie-journey with local delicacies and interesting guest appearances. From drinking beer with a German friend, catching crabs by the ocean and devouring grilled eel the classic Korean way, this episode will leave you hungry for more.

programme.title: Kung fu journey up Wudang Mountains
programme.desc: Located in central China's Hubei Province, Wudang Mountains is a UNESCO World Natural and Cultural Heritage site recognized as the cradle of Wudang kung fu and as a renowned Taoist sanctuary. Every year, young students from different parts of China flock to these well-established kung fu academies to experience a childhood like no other. Li Weimu and Li Muzi study at two separate kung fu academies, and are following their own paths at Wudang Mountains.

programme.title: Diabolo
programme.desc: In China, the diabolo has a history of over 600 years. From the earliest uniaxial design, it developed to have a double axis. With time, it came to be made in various shapes and from various materials. And its popularity began to spread around the world. In the hands of an expert, a diabolo can create a truly mesmerizing spectacle. So, let’s hear from a professional about the various tricks of his trade.

programme.title: From Stone to Story
programme.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programme.title: Huayin Old Tunes: the Earliest Rock and Roll by the Yellow River
programme.desc: Huayin Laoqiang is the name given to a traditional form of shadow puppet show. It translates as “Huayin Old Tunes,” and originated in the chanting of the boatmen working at the confluence of the Yellow, Weihe, and Luohe Rivers, close to the city of Huayin in Shaanxi Province. This chanting evolved into folk songs which, with time, were illustrated with puppet shows. The loud music, rustic instruments, and enthusiastic performances epitomize the way the local people lead their lives.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Colored stone inlay art: Transformation matters
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: The Story of Flying Trident
programme.desc: Feicha, or a flying trident, is a type of performance where you balance a revolving trident on your body. In Chinese history, the trident was one of the 18 main weapons of war, but now it has become more of an acrobatic act. For thousands of years, people in China have practiced it as a performance and also as a way to keep fit.\nIt seems like the trident is stuck to the person’s body like a magnet, but that effect is achieved by tensing and relaxing muscles at the right time so that the movement of the trident is channeled in the right direction—be it around the hands, neck, or back. Otherwise, its energy will turn against the performer.

programme.title: Awakening Lion
programme.desc: Xingshi, or literally “awakened lion,” is a name for the southern style of the lion dance. Historically, it evolved from a court dance in the Tang Dynasty almost 1,200 years ago. With the southward migration of the population from the central plains, lion dance culture was introduced to the south. In the Ming Dynasty around 400 years ago, xingshi appeared in Guangdong and was then widely spread throughout southern China and even overseas. Lion dance has become a very important part of Chinese culture.

programme.title: Foodwise: Beijing
programme.desc: From the local delicacies of Beijing's Ox Street to latest hangouts and contemporary cuisine of the Chinese capital, hosts Tianran He and Eva May will give you a true taste of Beijing, and the fascinating stories and people behind them.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of Tibetan antelopes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise: Beijing's Hutongs
programme.desc: The Hutongs in Beijing are considered the city's blood vessels, as these traditional alleyways make up the DNA of the capital for its rich history and culture.\nIn this episode, Tianran He and Eva May zigzag their way through the Hutongs and the Forbidden City for a mouth-watering experience full of ancient and modern delicacies. Starring German designer Katrin, Russian artist Liuba and photographer Feng Yu from Beijing, our hosts are taking you along a journey that features fried noodles, dim sum, pancakes and co.

programme.title: Foodwise: Changsha
programme.desc: In this episode, our hosts Tianran He and Eva May embark on a journey to Changsha in Hunan Province.?They taste their way through the local cuisine, discover the city's latest art and fashion trends, and find out why this place is one of China's \"influencer hotspots.

programme.title: Foodwise：Qingdao
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May venture to Qingdao, China's beer mecca by the sea. With red tiles and green trees that make up the city's stunning scenery, our hosts embark on another foodie-journey with local delicacies and interesting guest appearances. From drinking beer with a German friend, catching crabs by the ocean and devouring grilled eel the classic Korean way, this episode will leave you hungry for more.

programme.title: Kung fu journey up Wudang Mountains
programme.desc: Located in central China's Hubei Province, Wudang Mountains is a UNESCO World Natural and Cultural Heritage site recognized as the cradle of Wudang kung fu and as a renowned Taoist sanctuary. Every year, young students from different parts of China flock to these well-established kung fu academies to experience a childhood like no other. Li Weimu and Li Muzi study at two separate kung fu academies, and are following their own paths at Wudang Mountains.

programme.title: Diabolo
programme.desc: In China, the diabolo has a history of over 600 years. From the earliest uniaxial design, it developed to have a double axis. With time, it came to be made in various shapes and from various materials. And its popularity began to spread around the world. In the hands of an expert, a diabolo can create a truly mesmerizing spectacle. So, let’s hear from a professional about the various tricks of his trade.

programme.title: From Stone to Story
programme.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programme.title: Huayin Old Tunes: the Earliest Rock and Roll by the Yellow River
programme.desc: Huayin Laoqiang is the name given to a traditional form of shadow puppet show. It translates as “Huayin Old Tunes,” and originated in the chanting of the boatmen working at the confluence of the Yellow, Weihe, and Luohe Rivers, close to the city of Huayin in Shaanxi Province. This chanting evolved into folk songs which, with time, were illustrated with puppet shows. The loud music, rustic instruments, and enthusiastic performances epitomize the way the local people lead their lives.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Colored stone inlay art: Transformation matters
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: The Story of Flying Trident
programme.desc: Feicha, or a flying trident, is a type of performance where you balance a revolving trident on your body. In Chinese history, the trident was one of the 18 main weapons of war, but now it has become more of an acrobatic act. For thousands of years, people in China have practiced it as a performance and also as a way to keep fit.\nIt seems like the trident is stuck to the person’s body like a magnet, but that effect is achieved by tensing and relaxing muscles at the right time so that the movement of the trident is channeled in the right direction—be it around the hands, neck, or back. Otherwise, its energy will turn against the performer.

programme.title: Awakening Lion
programme.desc: Xingshi, or literally “awakened lion,” is a name for the southern style of the lion dance. Historically, it evolved from a court dance in the Tang Dynasty almost 1,200 years ago. With the southward migration of the population from the central plains, lion dance culture was introduced to the south. In the Ming Dynasty around 400 years ago, xingshi appeared in Guangdong and was then widely spread throughout southern China and even overseas. Lion dance has become a very important part of Chinese culture.

programme.title: Foodwise: Wuzhen
programme.desc: Join our hosts Tianran He and Eva May as they explore the boisterous event of Wuzhen's International Theatre festival, with colorful features of both theatrical and culinary nature. Foodie Tianran and art-lover Eva are accompanied by ubiquitous local street food, daily carnival performances and meet and greets with fascinating personalities. Let's set the stage for the beautiful merge of food and drama as they taste their way through the charming scene of Wuzhen.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of golden snub-nosed monkeys
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise：Eating Global in Beijing
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in search of global cuisines that can be found in Beijing, the 3000-year old capital of China. From honest farm-to-table fare, to the culinary heights of French haute cuisine, and even a cooking class and wine and dine event at a traditional courtyard home in the city center, we'll discover that in Beijing, you can taste the world.

programme.title: Foodwise: Quanzhou
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in Quanzhou, the ancient starting point of the Maritime Silk Road. From its covered arcades and Roman columns to its Southeast Asian vibe, our hosts discover that Quanzhou truly is a melting pot of world cultures. Then there’s the food: From the local noodle porridge that utilizes Silk Road spices, to fried vinegar pork and a dessert made from a special algae, there’s plenty of unique dishes to try here. Over a cup of oolong, our hosts will discover how Quanzhou’s tea changed the world, and how trade with Arab civilizations influenced a local fishing village. So join us in Quanzhou, a city that UNESCO designated as an “Emporium of the World”.

programme.title: Foodwise: Xi'an
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May venture to Xi'an, an ancient city with over 3000 years of history, capital to 13 Chinese dynasties. But first, our hosts are going to carb load on street food, from glutinous rice cakes and cakes made out of persimmons, to mutton soup with flatbread and the famous biangbiang noodles. They'll also taste the rich history of Xi'an by experiencing a Qin Dynasty-inspired banquet, and try millennia-old dishes such as trout grilled on a ceramic tile and calabash chicken, a Tang Dynasty delicacy.\nFrom the Terracotta Army to the Daming Palace, residence of the Tang emperors, to a dazzling nighttime boulevard that recreates the grandeur of the Tang Dynasty, there's so much to explore in Xi'an. So, get your camera and your stomach ready for CGTN's Foodwise in Xi'an.

programme.title: Breathing new life into century-old Yueju Opera
programme.desc: Yueju Opera is one of the five major Chinese operas. It is skillful in narration and sentiment and is popular across China. This timeless form of intangible cultural heritage now has new apprentices. We followed a young duo of Yueju Opera performers: eleven-year-old Wang Yuhan and her stage partner, nine-year-old Huang Xinru in Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, as they shared kindred spirits in inheriting the performing art of Yueju Opera.

programme.title: Wudang Martial Arts and Taichi
programme.desc: Join host Yegor on a journey into the heart of China’s Wudang Mountains—the spiritual cradle of Taoism and the birthplace of Wudang martial arts. Through in-depth conversations with renowned masters such as Zhong Xueyong, Cai Chongguang, Jake Pinnick, and Liu Xiang, this episode explores the philosophy, techniques, and living spirit of Wudang Quan and Taichi.\nFrom the internal cultivation of qi to the harmony between body and mind, Wudang martial arts are more than just physical training—they are a way of life rooted in the Tao. Alongside martial techniques, the program also delves into traditional Chinese music, calligraphy, and medicine, revealing how these disciplines come together in the pursuit of balance, self-awareness, and longevity.\nThis is not just a story of punches and postures, but of tradition, transformation, and the timeless search for harmony between humans and the universe.

programme.title: Suzhou Fans
programme.desc: Suzhou, known as the “Venice of China,” is also famous for its traditional crafts. And one of the most elegant and exquisite crafts is Suzhou fans. Celebrated for their exquisite design and fine quality, they’re popularly associated with literati. How come Suzhou gave birth to such a variety of fans? How can we appreciate their beauty in the modern world? In this episode, we are discovering more stories behind Suzhou fans.

programme.title: Pingyao Hand-Polished Lacquer Art
programme.desc: Pingyao is an old town with a history of over 2,700 years in the north of China, in Shanxi Province. The rich cultural heritage it built up over the centuries eventually gave rise to a very special art—Tuiguang lacquerware. This hand-polished lacquerware, unique to Pingyao, is recognized as one of the four representative lacquerwares of China.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Dance of Heroes
programme.desc: In this episode, our host “Uncle Hanzi” travels to Chaonan in Shantou to explore the story of a family devoted to the tradition of Yingge dance. Centered around inheritor Hong Shaohua, the story unfolds through his father’s dedication, his daughter’s passion, and his apprentices’ enthusiasm—showcasing how this centuries-old folk art continues to thrive in the modern world.

programme.title: China's Ancient “Wall Street”: Pingyao
programme.desc: In this episode, we head to Pingyao – the ancient \"Wall Street\" of China and a historic banking hub. Enclosed by ancient city walls and seemingly frozen in time, this entire city, together with two nearby ancient temples, offers a one-of-a-kind adventure at this UNESCO World Heritage Site.

programme.title: Foodwise: Wuzhen
programme.desc: Join our hosts Tianran He and Eva May as they explore the boisterous event of Wuzhen's International Theatre festival, with colorful features of both theatrical and culinary nature. Foodie Tianran and art-lover Eva are accompanied by ubiquitous local street food, daily carnival performances and meet and greets with fascinating personalities. Let's set the stage for the beautiful merge of food and drama as they taste their way through the charming scene of Wuzhen.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of golden snub-nosed monkeys
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise：Eating Global in Beijing
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in search of global cuisines that can be found in Beijing, the 3000-year old capital of China. From honest farm-to-table fare, to the culinary heights of French haute cuisine, and even a cooking class and wine and dine event at a traditional courtyard home in the city center, we'll discover that in Beijing, you can taste the world.

programme.title: Foodwise: Quanzhou
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in Quanzhou, the ancient starting point of the Maritime Silk Road. From its covered arcades and Roman columns to its Southeast Asian vibe, our hosts discover that Quanzhou truly is a melting pot of world cultures. Then there’s the food: From the local noodle porridge that utilizes Silk Road spices, to fried vinegar pork and a dessert made from a special algae, there’s plenty of unique dishes to try here. Over a cup of oolong, our hosts will discover how Quanzhou’s tea changed the world, and how trade with Arab civilizations influenced a local fishing village. So join us in Quanzhou, a city that UNESCO designated as an “Emporium of the World”.

programme.title: Foodwise: Xi'an
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May venture to Xi'an, an ancient city with over 3000 years of history, capital to 13 Chinese dynasties. But first, our hosts are going to carb load on street food, from glutinous rice cakes and cakes made out of persimmons, to mutton soup with flatbread and the famous biangbiang noodles. They'll also taste the rich history of Xi'an by experiencing a Qin Dynasty-inspired banquet, and try millennia-old dishes such as trout grilled on a ceramic tile and calabash chicken, a Tang Dynasty delicacy.\nFrom the Terracotta Army to the Daming Palace, residence of the Tang emperors, to a dazzling nighttime boulevard that recreates the grandeur of the Tang Dynasty, there's so much to explore in Xi'an. So, get your camera and your stomach ready for CGTN's Foodwise in Xi'an.

programme.title: Breathing new life into century-old Yueju Opera
programme.desc: Yueju Opera is one of the five major Chinese operas. It is skillful in narration and sentiment and is popular across China. This timeless form of intangible cultural heritage now has new apprentices. We followed a young duo of Yueju Opera performers: eleven-year-old Wang Yuhan and her stage partner, nine-year-old Huang Xinru in Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, as they shared kindred spirits in inheriting the performing art of Yueju Opera.

programme.title: Wudang Martial Arts and Taichi
programme.desc: Join host Yegor on a journey into the heart of China’s Wudang Mountains—the spiritual cradle of Taoism and the birthplace of Wudang martial arts. Through in-depth conversations with renowned masters such as Zhong Xueyong, Cai Chongguang, Jake Pinnick, and Liu Xiang, this episode explores the philosophy, techniques, and living spirit of Wudang Quan and Taichi.\nFrom the internal cultivation of qi to the harmony between body and mind, Wudang martial arts are more than just physical training—they are a way of life rooted in the Tao. Alongside martial techniques, the program also delves into traditional Chinese music, calligraphy, and medicine, revealing how these disciplines come together in the pursuit of balance, self-awareness, and longevity.\nThis is not just a story of punches and postures, but of tradition, transformation, and the timeless search for harmony between humans and the universe.

programme.title: Suzhou Fans
programme.desc: Suzhou, known as the “Venice of China,” is also famous for its traditional crafts. And one of the most elegant and exquisite crafts is Suzhou fans. Celebrated for their exquisite design and fine quality, they’re popularly associated with literati. How come Suzhou gave birth to such a variety of fans? How can we appreciate their beauty in the modern world? In this episode, we are discovering more stories behind Suzhou fans.

programme.title: Pingyao Hand-Polished Lacquer Art
programme.desc: Pingyao is an old town with a history of over 2,700 years in the north of China, in Shanxi Province. The rich cultural heritage it built up over the centuries eventually gave rise to a very special art—Tuiguang lacquerware. This hand-polished lacquerware, unique to Pingyao, is recognized as one of the four representative lacquerwares of China.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Dance of Heroes
programme.desc: In this episode, our host “Uncle Hanzi” travels to Chaonan in Shantou to explore the story of a family devoted to the tradition of Yingge dance. Centered around inheritor Hong Shaohua, the story unfolds through his father’s dedication, his daughter’s passion, and his apprentices’ enthusiasm—showcasing how this centuries-old folk art continues to thrive in the modern world.

programme.title: China's Ancient “Wall Street”: Pingyao
programme.desc: In this episode, we head to Pingyao – the ancient \"Wall Street\" of China and a historic banking hub. Enclosed by ancient city walls and seemingly frozen in time, this entire city, together with two nearby ancient temples, offers a one-of-a-kind adventure at this UNESCO World Heritage Site.

programme.title: Foodwise: Wuzhen
programme.desc: Join our hosts Tianran He and Eva May as they explore the boisterous event of Wuzhen's International Theatre festival, with colorful features of both theatrical and culinary nature. Foodie Tianran and art-lover Eva are accompanied by ubiquitous local street food, daily carnival performances and meet and greets with fascinating personalities. Let's set the stage for the beautiful merge of food and drama as they taste their way through the charming scene of Wuzhen.

programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise：Eating Global in Beijing
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in search of global cuisines that can be found in Beijing, the 3000-year old capital of China. From honest farm-to-table fare, to the culinary heights of French haute cuisine, and even a cooking class and wine and dine event at a traditional courtyard home in the city center, we'll discover that in Beijing, you can taste the world.

programme.title: Foodwise: Quanzhou
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in Quanzhou, the ancient starting point of the Maritime Silk Road. From its covered arcades and Roman columns to its Southeast Asian vibe, our hosts discover that Quanzhou truly is a melting pot of world cultures. Then there’s the food: From the local noodle porridge that utilizes Silk Road spices, to fried vinegar pork and a dessert made from a special algae, there’s plenty of unique dishes to try here. Over a cup of oolong, our hosts will discover how Quanzhou’s tea changed the world, and how trade with Arab civilizations influenced a local fishing village. So join us in Quanzhou, a city that UNESCO designated as an “Emporium of the World”.

programme.title: Foodwise: Xi'an
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May venture to Xi'an, an ancient city with over 3000 years of history, capital to 13 Chinese dynasties. But first, our hosts are going to carb load on street food, from glutinous rice cakes and cakes made out of persimmons, to mutton soup with flatbread and the famous biangbiang noodles. They'll also taste the rich history of Xi'an by experiencing a Qin Dynasty-inspired banquet, and try millennia-old dishes such as trout grilled on a ceramic tile and calabash chicken, a Tang Dynasty delicacy.\nFrom the Terracotta Army to the Daming Palace, residence of the Tang emperors, to a dazzling nighttime boulevard that recreates the grandeur of the Tang Dynasty, there's so much to explore in Xi'an. So, get your camera and your stomach ready for CGTN's Foodwise in Xi'an.

programme.title: Breathing new life into century-old Yueju Opera
programme.desc: Yueju Opera is one of the five major Chinese operas. It is skillful in narration and sentiment and is popular across China. This timeless form of intangible cultural heritage now has new apprentices. We followed a young duo of Yueju Opera performers: eleven-year-old Wang Yuhan and her stage partner, nine-year-old Huang Xinru in Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, as they shared kindred spirits in inheriting the performing art of Yueju Opera.

programme.title: Wudang Martial Arts and Taichi
programme.desc: Join host Yegor on a journey into the heart of China’s Wudang Mountains—the spiritual cradle of Taoism and the birthplace of Wudang martial arts. Through in-depth conversations with renowned masters such as Zhong Xueyong, Cai Chongguang, Jake Pinnick, and Liu Xiang, this episode explores the philosophy, techniques, and living spirit of Wudang Quan and Taichi.\nFrom the internal cultivation of qi to the harmony between body and mind, Wudang martial arts are more than just physical training—they are a way of life rooted in the Tao. Alongside martial techniques, the program also delves into traditional Chinese music, calligraphy, and medicine, revealing how these disciplines come together in the pursuit of balance, self-awareness, and longevity.\nThis is not just a story of punches and postures, but of tradition, transformation, and the timeless search for harmony between humans and the universe.

programme.title: Suzhou Fans
programme.desc: Suzhou, known as the “Venice of China,” is also famous for its traditional crafts. And one of the most elegant and exquisite crafts is Suzhou fans. Celebrated for their exquisite design and fine quality, they’re popularly associated with literati. How come Suzhou gave birth to such a variety of fans? How can we appreciate their beauty in the modern world? In this episode, we are discovering more stories behind Suzhou fans.

programme.title: Pingyao Hand-Polished Lacquer Art
programme.desc: Pingyao is an old town with a history of over 2,700 years in the north of China, in Shanxi Province. The rich cultural heritage it built up over the centuries eventually gave rise to a very special art—Tuiguang lacquerware. This hand-polished lacquerware, unique to Pingyao, is recognized as one of the four representative lacquerwares of China.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Dance of Heroes
programme.desc: In this episode, our host “Uncle Hanzi” travels to Chaonan in Shantou to explore the story of a family devoted to the tradition of Yingge dance. Centered around inheritor Hong Shaohua, the story unfolds through his father’s dedication, his daughter’s passion, and his apprentices’ enthusiasm—showcasing how this centuries-old folk art continues to thrive in the modern world.

programme.title: China's Ancient “Wall Street”: Pingyao
programme.desc: In this episode, we head to Pingyao – the ancient \"Wall Street\" of China and a historic banking hub. Enclosed by ancient city walls and seemingly frozen in time, this entire city, together with two nearby ancient temples, offers a one-of-a-kind adventure at this UNESCO World Heritage Site.

programme.title: Foodwise: Wuzhen
programme.desc: Join our hosts Tianran He and Eva May as they explore the boisterous event of Wuzhen's International Theatre festival, with colorful features of both theatrical and culinary nature. Foodie Tianran and art-lover Eva are accompanied by ubiquitous local street food, daily carnival performances and meet and greets with fascinating personalities. Let's set the stage for the beautiful merge of food and drama as they taste their way through the charming scene of Wuzhen.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of golden snub-nosed monkeys
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise：Eating Global in Beijing
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in search of global cuisines that can be found in Beijing, the 3000-year old capital of China. From honest farm-to-table fare, to the culinary heights of French haute cuisine, and even a cooking class and wine and dine event at a traditional courtyard home in the city center, we'll discover that in Beijing, you can taste the world.

programme.title: Foodwise: Quanzhou
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May are in Quanzhou, the ancient starting point of the Maritime Silk Road. From its covered arcades and Roman columns to its Southeast Asian vibe, our hosts discover that Quanzhou truly is a melting pot of world cultures. Then there’s the food: From the local noodle porridge that utilizes Silk Road spices, to fried vinegar pork and a dessert made from a special algae, there’s plenty of unique dishes to try here. Over a cup of oolong, our hosts will discover how Quanzhou’s tea changed the world, and how trade with Arab civilizations influenced a local fishing village. So join us in Quanzhou, a city that UNESCO designated as an “Emporium of the World”.

programme.title: Foodwise: Xi'an
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Eva May venture to Xi'an, an ancient city with over 3000 years of history, capital to 13 Chinese dynasties. But first, our hosts are going to carb load on street food, from glutinous rice cakes and cakes made out of persimmons, to mutton soup with flatbread and the famous biangbiang noodles. They'll also taste the rich history of Xi'an by experiencing a Qin Dynasty-inspired banquet, and try millennia-old dishes such as trout grilled on a ceramic tile and calabash chicken, a Tang Dynasty delicacy.\nFrom the Terracotta Army to the Daming Palace, residence of the Tang emperors, to a dazzling nighttime boulevard that recreates the grandeur of the Tang Dynasty, there's so much to explore in Xi'an. So, get your camera and your stomach ready for CGTN's Foodwise in Xi'an.

programme.title: Breathing new life into century-old Yueju Opera
programme.desc: Yueju Opera is one of the five major Chinese operas. It is skillful in narration and sentiment and is popular across China. This timeless form of intangible cultural heritage now has new apprentices. We followed a young duo of Yueju Opera performers: eleven-year-old Wang Yuhan and her stage partner, nine-year-old Huang Xinru in Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, as they shared kindred spirits in inheriting the performing art of Yueju Opera.

programme.title: Wudang Martial Arts and Taichi
programme.desc: Join host Yegor on a journey into the heart of China’s Wudang Mountains—the spiritual cradle of Taoism and the birthplace of Wudang martial arts. Through in-depth conversations with renowned masters such as Zhong Xueyong, Cai Chongguang, Jake Pinnick, and Liu Xiang, this episode explores the philosophy, techniques, and living spirit of Wudang Quan and Taichi.\nFrom the internal cultivation of qi to the harmony between body and mind, Wudang martial arts are more than just physical training—they are a way of life rooted in the Tao. Alongside martial techniques, the program also delves into traditional Chinese music, calligraphy, and medicine, revealing how these disciplines come together in the pursuit of balance, self-awareness, and longevity.\nThis is not just a story of punches and postures, but of tradition, transformation, and the timeless search for harmony between humans and the universe.

programme.title: Suzhou Fans
programme.desc: Suzhou, known as the “Venice of China,” is also famous for its traditional crafts. And one of the most elegant and exquisite crafts is Suzhou fans. Celebrated for their exquisite design and fine quality, they’re popularly associated with literati. How come Suzhou gave birth to such a variety of fans? How can we appreciate their beauty in the modern world? In this episode, we are discovering more stories behind Suzhou fans.

programme.title: Pingyao Hand-Polished Lacquer Art
programme.desc: Pingyao is an old town with a history of over 2,700 years in the north of China, in Shanxi Province. The rich cultural heritage it built up over the centuries eventually gave rise to a very special art—Tuiguang lacquerware. This hand-polished lacquerware, unique to Pingyao, is recognized as one of the four representative lacquerwares of China.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Dance of Heroes
programme.desc: In this episode, our host “Uncle Hanzi” travels to Chaonan in Shantou to explore the story of a family devoted to the tradition of Yingge dance. Centered around inheritor Hong Shaohua, the story unfolds through his father’s dedication, his daughter’s passion, and his apprentices’ enthusiasm—showcasing how this centuries-old folk art continues to thrive in the modern world.

programme.title: China's Ancient “Wall Street”: Pingyao
programme.desc: In this episode, we head to Pingyao – the ancient \"Wall Street\" of China and a historic banking hub. Enclosed by ancient city walls and seemingly frozen in time, this entire city, together with two nearby ancient temples, offers a one-of-a-kind adventure at this UNESCO World Heritage Site.

programme.title: Foodwise: Datong
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Angela Fu are in historic Datong. Located in northern China's Shanxi Province, some 1500 years ago Datong was the capital of the Northern Wei Dynasty, which saw nomads unite northern China and Buddhism spread throughout the country. At the renowned Yungang Grottoes where giant Buddhas were carved out of cliffs, our hosts discover Datong's multicultural past and sample the emperor's favorite cake. They seek out the remains of a Jesuit church beneath the Great Wall of China, and find in the local cuisine elements that echo the past, and modern-day recipes for thousand-year old dishes.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of black-necked cranes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise: Chaozhou
programme.desc: In this episode, hosts Tianran He and Angela Fu are in Chaozhou, South China. They're on a quest to discover the secrets of its success, for Chaozhou is famed for its wealthy merchant guilds and its homegrown Teochew cuisine, which enjoys global recognition.\nFrom working as part-timers in a beef hotpot restaurant, to learning about tea ceremony and shadowing a master of Teochew cuisine, our hosts discover the best way to learn about Chaozhou, is through its food.

programme.title: Foodwise: Chongqing
programme.desc: Welcome to Chongqing, China's largest city. Located in the country's southwest, it straddles two mighty rivers and is famed for its extraordinary skyline, with high-rises mushrooming out of the hills and vertigo-inducing bridges.But Tianran He and Angela Fu are here for the food: From xiaomian noodles and Jianghu cuisine to the city's most famous dish: Chongqing hotpot, our hosts take on the spicy, numbing and scintillating aromas of Chongqing cuisine. We promise you: Chongqing will electrify your senses and tantalise your taste buds.

programme.title: Foodwise: Veggie Beijing
programme.desc: Beijing is a 3000-year old metropolis home to a dizzying array of culinary traditions, not least of which is vegetarian cuisine. \nFor Beijingers, eating vegetarian is the best way to welcome spring. In this episode, Tianran and Angela take up the challenge: From simple home cooking utilizing spring flowers and leaves, to the extravagant mock-meat masterpieces of imperial cuisine, and international dishes well-known to the vegetarian community, our hosts discover a plethora of plant-based options to be found amongst the historic alleys and skyscrapers of China's capital.

programme.title: Painting beyond the spectrum
programme.desc: In many ways, 12-year-old Zhu Jingxiu is just like any other boy his age. He is skilled in drawing, enjoys playing the piano, loves to ride his bicycle, and likes to use his mobile phone to film videos of traffic and skyscrapers.

programme.title: The Tongliang Dragon Dance
programme.desc: The Tongliang Dragon Dance is a traditional performance art rooted in Chongqing’s Tongliang District, characterized by its elaborate dragon props and dynamic choreography. Originating in the Ming Dynasty and flourishing during the Qing, this vibrant folk tradition has regained its brilliance in modern times and earned international acclaim. The dance consists of two main branches: the dragon lantern dance—featuring forms like the Grand Wriggling Dragon, the Fire Dragon, and the Straw Dragon—and the festive lantern dance, which includes acts such as “Carp Leaps Over the Dragon Gate,” “Mudfish Eating Dumplings,” and “Three Dragons.” Steeped in cultural symbolism, the Tongliang Dragon Dance expresses values of unity, vitality, and harmony with nature. While a few iconic forms like the Fire Dragon remain widely recognized, many others are on the verge of extinction and in urgent need of preservation. In this episode, the host travels to Chongqing to meet with four inheritors of the Tongliang Dragon Dance. Through their stories, we uncover the rich legacy of this dazzling tradition, explore the deep roots of dragon culture in the region, and trace the origins of one of China’s most awe-inspiring folk arts.

programme.title: Ding Porcelain Firing
programme.desc:
In ancient times, skilled craftsmen combined clay with fire and, using nothing more than their hands and wisdom, created the history of porcelain.

A thousand years ago, at the time of the Northern Song Dynasty, a type of porcelain was produced that stood out for its exquisite quality, elegant coloring and beautiful patterns. This was Ding porcelain, and it became a favorite at the imperial court. However, within 200 years, amid the chaos of constant warfare, the art of making it was lost. The dazzling white light of the Ding kilns had faded, seemingly forever. 

And yet, 800 years later, that light has been reignited.

programme.title: Seafaring Tales: Fu Ships
programme.desc: In past centuries, transporting goods along the Maritime Silk Road could be a hazardous undertaking. Ships often faced fierce storms which blew up on the high seas. To reduce the danger, China’s shipbuilders came up with a solution. They developed Fu ships, commonly known as junks, incorporating a special watertight bulkhead technology. Their revolutionary design allowed the ships to withstand the highest winds and roughest waters.\nThe Maritime Silk Road was an ancient trading route that connected China to Southeast and South Asia, the Middle East, and Africa. As a key passageway for trade and cultural exchange, it thrived for well over a thousand years, bringing Chinese tea, silk, porcelain, spices, and other precious cargo to the rest of the planet. The legacy of the Maritime Silk Road is still evident today in many parts of the world.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS “Tribute tea” to more tea lovers
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Jingdezhen Handmade Porcelain
programme.desc: Porcelain has shaped the culture of the whole world. The wisdom behind each of 72 procedures has been refined from generation to generation. The times may change, some techniques may change, but what remains the same is the burning flame of the kiln that keeps setting so many hearts on fire for this ancient craft.

programme.title: A Vibe of the Village: The Beat of Xiangxi Drums
programme.desc: Experience the vibrant power of Xiangxi Drum Dance. This program delves into the heart of Tujia and Miao cultures, showcasing the rhythmic beats and spirited performances that have echoed through West Hunan's mountains for centuries. Discover the stories, traditions, and raw energy of this captivating folk art.

programme.title: Foodwise: Datong
programme.desc: In this episode, Tianran He and Angela Fu are in historic Datong. Located in northern China's Shanxi Province, some 1500 years ago Datong was the capital of the Northern Wei Dynasty, which saw nomads unite northern China and Buddhism spread throughout the country. At the renowned Yungang Grottoes where giant Buddhas were carved out of cliffs, our hosts discover Datong's multicultural past and sample the emperor's favorite cake. They seek out the remains of a Jesuit church beneath the Great Wall of China, and find in the local cuisine elements that echo the past, and modern-day recipes for thousand-year old dishes.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of black-necked cranes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Foodwise: Chaozhou
programme.desc: In this episode, hosts Tianran He and Angela Fu are in Chaozhou, South China. They're on a quest to discover the secrets of its success, for Chaozhou is famed for its wealthy merchant guilds and its homegrown Teochew cuisine, which enjoys global recognition.\nFrom working as part-timers in a beef hotpot restaurant, to learning about tea ceremony and shadowing a master of Teochew cuisine, our hosts discover the best way to learn about Chaozhou, is through its food.

programme.title: Foodwise: Chongqing
programme.desc: Welcome to Chongqing, China's largest city. Located in the country's southwest, it straddles two mighty rivers and is famed for its extraordinary skyline, with high-rises mushrooming out of the hills and vertigo-inducing bridges.But Tianran He and Angela Fu are here for the food: From xiaomian noodles and Jianghu cuisine to the city's most famous dish: Chongqing hotpot, our hosts take on the spicy, numbing and scintillating aromas of Chongqing cuisine. We promise you: Chongqing will electrify your senses and tantalise your taste buds.
